message:
feat(core): asupersync Cx capability-context adapters (wa-2lp7o)

Add the `cx` module (feature-gated behind `asupersync-runtime`) providing
FrankenTerm-facing adapters for threading `asupersync::Cx` through async
call graphs during the migration away from ambient runtime access.

Key components:
- `Cx` re-export and `CxRuntimeBuilder` for constructing runtimes with
  budget/quota support and tunable thread parameters (`RuntimeTuning`)
- `with_cx()` for synchronous Cx propagation through layered call chains
- `with_cx_async()` for async Cx propagation in futures
- `spawn_with_cx()` / `try_spawn_with_cx()` for spawning tasks that
  inherit the parent Cx context
- `for_testing()` helper that constructs a Cx with generous defaults
  suitable for unit/integration tests

Also includes:
- `cx_propagation` benchmark measuring context-threading overhead at
  call depths 1/5/10/20 with various budget configurations
- Integration tests verifying checkpoint propagation, budget exhaustion,
  spawn behavior, and panic isolation
- Property-based tests (proptest) validating that Cx propagation at
  arbitrary depths preserves checkpoint invariants and that budget
  bounds are respected across randomized configurations

This establishes the explicit capability-threading pattern required
before Phase 2 (wa-3d14m) can replace tokio sync primitives with
asupersync equivalents.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-5ap","title":"Notification history: persistent log with queries, retry, and retention","description":"# Notification History: Persistent log of sent notifications

## Purpose
Track all sent notifications for debugging, auditing, and user review.

## Implementation

### Database Schema
```sql
CREATE TABLE notification_history (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    timestamp TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now')),

    -- Event reference
    event_id INTEGER REFERENCES events(id),

    -- Notification details
    channel TEXT NOT NULL,  -- 'webhook', 'desktop', 'slack', etc.
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    body TEXT NOT NULL,
    severity TEXT NOT NULL,  -- 'info', 'warning', 'error', 'critical'

    -- Delivery status
    status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',  -- 'pending', 'sent', 'failed', 'throttled'
    error_message TEXT,

    -- Response tracking
    acknowledged_at TEXT,
    acknowledged_by TEXT,
    action_taken TEXT,

    -- Metadata
    metadata TEXT  -- JSON blob for channel-specific data
);

CREATE INDEX idx_notification_history_timestamp ON notification_history(timestamp);
CREATE INDEX idx_notification_history_status ON notification_history(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_notification_history_event ON notification_history(event_id);
```

### CLI Commands
```bash
# List recent notifications
$ wa notifications list
ID     Time       Channel   Event                        Status
-----  ---------  --------  ---------------------------  -------
123    14:30:15   webhook   codex.usage_limit_reached    sent
122    14:28:03   desktop   session.compaction           sent
121    14:25:00   webhook   codex.session_summary        throttled

# Show notification details
$ wa notifications show 123
Notification #123
  Time: 2026-01-18T14:30:15Z
  Channel: webhook
  Event: codex.usage_limit_reached (event #456)
  Status: sent

  Title: wa: codex.usage_limit_reached
  Body:
    Detected: codex.usage_limit_reached
    Pane: 9 (codex @ /project)
    ...

# Filter by status/channel
$ wa notifications list --status=failed
$ wa notifications list --channel=webhook --since=1h

# Retry failed notifications
$ wa notifications retry 121
```

### Robot Mode
```bash
$ wa robot notifications --limit 10 --format json
{
  "notifications": [
    {
      "id": 123,
      "timestamp": "2026-01-18T14:30:15Z",
      "channel": "webhook",
      "event_id": 456,
      "rule_id": "codex.usage_limit_reached",
      "status": "sent",
      "acknowledged": false
    }
  ]
}
```

### History Queries
```rust
pub struct NotificationHistoryQuery {
    pub since: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
    pub until: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
    pub channel: Option<String>,
    pub status: Option<NotificationStatus>,
    pub event_id: Option<i64>,
    pub limit: usize,
}

impl NotificationHistory {
    pub fn query(&self, q: NotificationHistoryQuery) -> Result<Vec<NotificationRecord>> {
        // Build and execute query
    }

    pub fn record_sent(&self, notification: &Notification) -> Result<i64> {
        // Insert sent notification
    }

    pub fn record_failure(&self, id: i64, error: &str) -> Result<()> {
        // Update status to failed
    }

    pub fn mark_acknowledged(&self, id: i64, by: &str, action: &str) -> Result<()> {
        // Record acknowledgment
    }
}
```

### Retention Policy
```toml
[notifications.history]
# Keep notification history for 30 days
retention_days = 30

# Keep failed notifications longer for debugging
failed_retention_days = 90

# Prune on startup
auto_prune = true
```

## Testing
- Notifications recorded with correct details
- Query filters work correctly
- Retry re-sends failed notifications
- Retention pruning works

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] notification_history table created
- [ ] wa notifications list/show commands
- [ ] Robot mode JSON output
- [ ] Retry mechanism for failed notifications
- [ ] Retention policy with auto-prune

 {"id":"wa-5da3j","title":"Phase 3: Replace tokio network I/O with asupersync","description":"# Phase 3: Replace tokio network I/O

## Goal
Replace all tokio network I/O (Unix sockets, TCP, TLS) with asupersync networking primitives.

## Scope
- tokio::net::UnixStream/UnixListener → asupersync::net equivalents
- tokio::net::TcpListener/TcpStream → asupersync::net::TcpListener/TcpStream
- tokio-rustls → asupersync TLS (rustls-based)
- tokio::io::{AsyncReadExt, AsyncWriteExt, BufReader} → asupersync I/O traits
- reqwest → asupersync::http client

## Key architectural note
asupersync provides its own networking stack that does NOT depend on tokio. The I/O reactor abstracts over epoll/kqueue/io_uring. All network ops go through Cx capability context.

## Affected modules
- mux_client.rs — UnixStream (997 LOC, most critical)
- ipc.rs — UnixListener + UnixStream (~400 LOC)
- native_events.rs — UnixListener (~400 LOC)
- web.rs — TcpListener (feature-gated)
- distributed.rs — TLS/mTLS (feature-gated)

## Migration risk: HIGH
Network I/O is where most subtle bugs hide. The mux_client.rs PDU framing protocol over Unix sockets is especially delicate.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:48.625855Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:33.55915Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:33.559135Z","close_reason":"Flattened: Phase 3 was a pure bottleneck with no concrete work. Its children (wa-q8vj3 Unix sockets, wa-1u55z TCP/TLS) now depend directly on their prerequisites (wa-2abzy channels, wa-1yz79 time). Network I/O summary preserved in wa-e34d9 epic."}
 {"id":"wa-5em","title":"[EPIC] Rollback Log Visualization: trace and visualize workflow undo capabilities","description":"# [EPIC] Rollback Log Visualization

## Mission
Provide visibility into what actions wa has taken and what could be undone, building trust through transparency.

## Why This Matters
Users fear automation because they don't know:
- What actions have been taken
- Whether actions can be undone
- How to recover from mistakes

Rollback visualization addresses:
- **Trust**: See exactly what wa did
- **Recovery**: Know what can be undone
- **Debugging**: Trace action sequences

## Scope

### Action Log View
- Timeline of all wa actions
- Grouped by workflow/pane
- Undo status for each action

### Visualization
- `wa history` - action timeline
- `wa history --pane 0` - pane-specific history
- `wa history --undo-able` - only undoable actions

### Undo Capabilities
- Send actions: show what was sent (can't undo)
- Workflow states: can abort/resume
- File changes (if any): can restore

### Integration
- Works with audit trail
- Links to workflow step logs
- Shows in TUI

## Success Criteria
- Users can see full action history
- Undo status is clear
- Recovery paths documented

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa history shows action timeline
- [ ] Undo status displayed per action
- [ ] Grouping by workflow works
- [ ] Integration with audit trail
- [ ] Tests cover visualization

## Testing
- Unit tests for undoability classification.
- Integration tests for history query joins.
- E2E scenario captures history output and logs.

 {"id":"wa-5em.1","title":"Action history model + undoability classification","description":"# Task: Action history model + undoability classification

## Goal
Define a **structured action history** that joins audit trail + workflow step logs and marks whether actions are undoable.

## Scope
- Merge audit_actions + workflow_step_log into a unified “history view” model.
- Classify each action with:
  - `undoable: bool`
  - `undo_strategy` (e.g., "manual", "resume", "none")
  - `undo_hint` (human‑readable guidance)

## Deliverables
- A read‑only view/query that joins:
  - audit trail entries
  - workflow step metadata
  - pane/workflow identifiers
- A deterministic undoability classifier with explicit rules.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - classification rules for common action kinds (send_text, workflow_step, approval)
  - join logic returns stable ordering

## Acceptance Criteria
- All action types are classified with an undoability state.
- History model can be queried per pane or workflow.

 {"id":"wa-y2e","title":"Robot mode dry-run: wa robot … --dry-run previews (send/workflow/events)","description":"# Robot mode dry-run previews (`wa robot … --dry-run`)

## Goal
Extend dry-run preview semantics to robot-mode commands so an AI agent can plan safely before taking actions.

This task is specifically about **robot mode** (machine output), and complements the **human** dry-run work in `wa-1pe.*`.

## Scope
Add `--dry-run` to robot commands that would otherwise mutate state:
- `wa robot send --dry-run …`
- `wa robot workflow … --dry-run`

Also add “planning previews” for read-only commands that *cause the agent to act*:
- `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run` (preview which workflows would run, without running them)

Non-goals (for v0.1):
- actually granting approvals from robot mode (approval is intentionally a human/interactive surface)

## Output contract
- Uses the standard robot envelope (`wa-4vx.7.1`, schemas in `wa-4vx.7.10`).
- Must be deterministic and redacted.
- Must never:
  - send text
  - acquire locks/reservations
  - mutate DB state (other than unavoidable read-side effects like opening the DB)

## Examples

### `wa robot send --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "send",
    "dry_run": true,
    "target": {
      "pane_id": 3,
      "title": "codex @ /project",
      "agent_type": "codex"
    },
    "input": {
      "text_preview": "continue",
      "text_len": 8
    },
    "policy_evaluation": {
      "decision": "allow",
      "capabilities": {
        "prompt_active": true,
        "command_running": false,
        "alt_screen": false,
        "recent_gap": false
      },
      "command_safety": {
        "is_command_candidate": false,
        "decision": "allow"
      }
    },
    "would_execute": true
  },
  "error": null
}
```

### `wa robot workflow run --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "workflow_run",
    "dry_run": true,
    "workflow": "handle_compaction",
    "target": {"pane_id": 3, "title": "claude @ /project"},
    "steps_preview": [
      {"step": 1, "name": "verify_compaction_state", "kind": "check_pattern"},
      {"step": 2, "name": "inject_context", "kind": "send_text", "text_preview": "Please re-read …"},
      {"step": 3, "name": "wait_ack", "kind": "wait_for", "pattern": "I've reviewed"}
    ],
    "estimated_duration_ms": 30000
  },
  "error": null
}
```

### `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "events",
    "dry_run": true,
    "events": [
      {
        "event_id": 123,
        "rule_id": "codex.usage_limit_reached",
        "pane_id": 9,
        "would_handle_with": {
          "workflow": "handle_usage_limits",
          "first_step": "exit_codex",
          "estimated_duration_ms": 60000
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "error": null
}
```

## Design notes
- Robot dry-run should reuse the same planning logic as human dry-run where possible:
  - shared `DryRunReport` structs (different renderers)
  - same PolicyEngine evaluation paths
- If `--dry-run` is combined with `--wait-for` style arguments, the preview should include:
  - what matcher would be used
  - timeout/backoff parameters
  - but must not actually wait

## Testing
- Unit/contract tests:
  - `--dry-run` never calls SendText / workflow execution
  - outputs validate against JSON schemas (`wa-4vx.7.10`)
  - redaction invariants hold (no secrets in previews)

- E2E:
  - add an adapter case to the E2E registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`) that runs:
    - `wa robot send --dry-run …`
    - `wa robot workflow … --dry-run`
    - `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run`
  - assert:
    - outputs are parseable JSON
    - `dry_run: true`
    - no audit “action executed” rows were created

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `wa robot send --dry-run` returns a deterministic, schema-valid preview including policy evaluation.
- [ ] `wa robot workflow … --dry-run` returns a deterministic, schema-valid step preview.
- [ ] `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run` previews which workflows would run.
- [ ] Dry-run paths do not acquire locks or mutate panes/DB state.

 {"id":"wa-y3gp","title":"Design: storage/indexing perf targets, metrics, and budgets","description":"# Task: Design storage/indexing perf targets

## Goal
Define measurable targets and budgets for storage and indexing.

## Requirements
- Define scale targets:
  - panes: N (e.g., 50+)
  - transcript size: M (e.g., multi-GB)
  - ingest rate: bytes/sec
- Define latency budgets:
  - append segment p95
  - FTS query p95 for common queries
  - indexing lag ceiling
- Define health metrics:
  - writer queue depth
  - WAL size / checkpoint cadence
  - index progress per pane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Targets are explicit and can be enforced by benchmarks and/or E2E.

 {"id":"wa-y3lz","title":"Smart command aliases: built-in short forms + user-configurable aliases","description":"
# Smart Command Aliases

## Purpose
Provide muscle-memory-friendly shortcuts for common operations.

## Built-in Aliases
```
was  → wa status
wae  → wa events --unhandled
waq  → wa query
waw  → wa watch
wat  → wa timeline
wad  → wa doctor
```

## Implementation
In wa.toml:
```toml
[aliases]
s = "status"
e = "events --unhandled"
q = "query"
t = "timeline"
d = "doctor"
```

In code:
```rust
fn resolve_alias(args: &[String], config: &Config) -> Vec<String> {
    if let Some(alias) = config.aliases.get(&args[0]) {
        let expanded = shell_words::split(alias)?;
        [expanded, args[1..].to_vec()].concat()
    } else {
        args.to_vec()
    }
}
```

## Shell Aliases (Optional)
```bash
# Add to .bashrc
alias was='wa status'
alias wae='wa events --unhandled'
```

Generated by: `wa setup aliases bash >> ~/.bashrc`

## Listing Aliases
```
$ wa aliases
Built-in:
  s  → status
  e  → events --unhandled
  ...

User-defined:
  x  → export --format=jsonl
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Built-in aliases work
- [ ] User aliases configurable in wa.toml
- [ ] `wa aliases` lists all aliases
- [ ] Shell alias generation helper
- [ ] No conflicts with subcommand names

## Testing
- Unit tests for completion/alias generation and help formatting.
- Integration tests for help output snapshots and no-ANSI guarantees.
- CI smoke tests for completions and help stability.

 {"id":"wa-y59","title":"Secure file permissions: restrictive umask for DB, logs, sockets","description":"# Task: Secure File Permissions

## Goal
Ensure wa files are created with appropriate permissions to prevent unauthorized access.

## Files Requiring Protection
1. **Database (wa.db)**: Contains terminal transcripts - may include secrets typed by user
2. **Logs**: May contain debugging info that includes sensitive paths/commands
3. **IPC sockets**: Used for inter-process communication
4. **Config files**: May contain webhook URLs with tokens
5. **Backup files**: Contains all above

## Implementation

### Permission Requirements
| File Type | Unix Mode | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Database | 0600 | Owner read/write only |
| Logs | 0600 | Owner read/write only |
| IPC socket | 0600 | Owner only |
| Lock files | 0644 | Others can check existence |
| Config | 0600 | May contain secrets |
| .wa directory | 0700 | Owner traverse only |

### Implementation
```rust
use std::os::unix::fs::PermissionsExt;

pub fn create_secure_file(path: &Path) -> io::Result<File> {
    let file = File::create(path)?;
    let mut perms = file.metadata()?.permissions();
    perms.set_mode(0o600);
    file.set_permissions(perms)?;
    Ok(file)
}

pub fn create_secure_directory(path: &Path) -> io::Result<()> {
    fs::create_dir_all(path)?;
    let mut perms = fs::metadata(path)?.permissions();
    perms.set_mode(0o700);
    fs::set_permissions(path, perms)?;
    Ok(())
}
```

### Startup Validation
On startup, check existing file permissions and warn if too permissive:
```
WARNING: Database permissions too open (0644)
  File: /home/user/.wa/wa.db
  Recommended: chmod 600 /home/user/.wa/wa.db
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: file creation with correct permissions
- Integration: wa doctor checks permissions
- E2E: new installation creates secure files

## Acceptance Criteria
- All sensitive files created with restrictive permissions
- wa doctor warns about insecure permissions
- Documentation explains security model","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:59:49.464206636Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:47.04551877Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:47.045439511Z","close_reason":"Implementation verified complete: Secure file permissions throughout codebase. storage.rs:977-1001 - set_permissions() creates DB/WAL/SHM files with 0o600, parent dirs with 0o700. logging.rs:119-161 - set_file_permissions() for log files at 0o600, log dirs at 0o700. config.rs:1662-1692 - check_permission() in wa doctor validates all sensitive paths (workspace dir, logs dir, crash dir, db, log files, ipc socket, config). Tests at storage.rs:5449-5459 and config.rs:2377-2402 verify permissions. main.rs:3106 outputs chmod recommendations for insecure files."}
 {"id":"wa-y6g","title":"Schema migration framework: versioned migrations with rollback support","description":"# Task: Schema Migration Framework

## Goal
Enable wa to safely upgrade its database schema across versions without data loss.

## Why This Matters
As wa evolves:
- New columns/tables needed for features
- Indexes may change for performance
- Data format may evolve

Without migrations, users would need to:
- Delete database and lose history
- Manually transform data

## Design

### Migration Files
```
src/storage/migrations/
├── mod.rs
├── v001_initial_schema.rs
├── v002_add_event_metadata.rs
├── v003_fts_improvements.rs
└── v004_workflow_state.rs
```

### Migration Structure
```rust
pub struct Migration {
    pub version: u32,
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub up: fn(&Connection) -> Result<()>,
    pub down: Option<fn(&Connection) -> Result<()>>,
}

// Example migration
pub static V002_ADD_EVENT_METADATA: Migration = Migration {
    version: 2,
    name: "add_event_metadata",
    up: |conn| {
        conn.execute_batch("
            ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN metadata TEXT;
            CREATE INDEX idx_events_metadata ON events(json_extract(metadata, '$.type'));
        ")?;
        Ok(())
    },
    down: Some(|conn| {
        conn.execute_batch("
            DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_events_metadata;
            ALTER TABLE events DROP COLUMN metadata;
        ")?;
        Ok(())
    }),
};
```

### Migration Runner
```rust
pub struct Migrator {
    migrations: Vec<&'static Migration>,
}

impl Migrator {
    pub fn run(&self, conn: &Connection) -> Result<MigrationReport> {
        let current = self.get_current_version(conn)?;
        let target = self.latest_version();
        
        tracing::info\!(
            current_version = current,
            target_version = target,
            "Starting migration"
        );
        
        if current == target {
            return Ok(MigrationReport::AlreadyCurrent);
        }
        
        // Run migrations in transaction
        conn.execute("BEGIN EXCLUSIVE", [])?;
        
        for migration in &self.migrations {
            if migration.version > current {
                tracing::info\!(
                    version = migration.version,
                    name = migration.name,
                    "Running migration"
                );
                
                (migration.up)(conn)?;
                self.record_migration(conn, migration)?;
            }
        }
        
        conn.execute("COMMIT", [])?;
        
        Ok(MigrationReport::Migrated {
            from: current,
            to: target,
        })
    }
}
```

### CLI Integration
```bash
# Automatic on startup (default)
$ wa watch
Migrating database: v1 → v3
  Running: v002_add_event_metadata... OK
  Running: v003_fts_improvements... OK
Migration complete.

# Manual migration
$ wa db migrate
Current version: 1
Target version: 3
Run migrations? [y/N]

# Check status without migrating
$ wa db migrate --status
Current version: 1
Available migrations:
  ✓ v001_initial_schema (applied)
  ○ v002_add_event_metadata (pending)
  ○ v003_fts_improvements (pending)
```

## Safety
- All migrations run in transaction
- Backup created before migration (if large changes)
- Down migrations available for rollback
- Version tracked in dedicated table

## Testing
- Unit tests: each migration up/down
- Integration: migrate from v1 to vN
- E2E: upgrade simulation with real data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Migrations run automatically on startup
- Failed migration rolls back cleanly
- Migration status visible via CLI
- Down migrations available for emergencies

 {"id":"wa-y8kz","title":"TUI scaffolding (charmed_rust): feature flag + app skeleton + shared query client","description":"# Task: TUI scaffolding (charmed_rust, feature tui)

## Goal
Create an **optional** interactive UI entrypoint: `wa tui`, behind `--features tui`, that provides a foundation for panes/events/search views.

The purpose of this bead is the **app skeleton** and **data access architecture**, not the final UX polish.

## Architecture (critical: keep UI dumb)
- Implement a small TUI app framework layer (initial screen router + event loop).
- Define a `QueryClient` trait (exact name flexible) used by the TUI:
  - `list_panes()`
  - `list_events(filters)`
  - `search(query)`
- Provide a production implementation backed by existing wa query surfaces:
  - prefer calling the same internal query/model layer that powers `wa robot ...`
  - never query SQLite directly from UI widgets

This separation is what makes the TUI testable and prevents “UI spaghetti + DB coupling”.

## UX baseline (v0)
- Global keybindings:
  - `q` quit
  - `?` help
  - `r` refresh
  - `tab` / `shift+tab` switch views (or a visible nav)
- Clear empty/error states:
  - watcher not running
  - DB not reachable
  - permission/policy denied

## Logging & diagnostics
- Use the structured logging baseline (tracing spans) so TUI bugs can be debugged.
- Avoid logging transcript contents; log counts/ids and redacted summaries only.

## Testing strategy
- `cargo test --features tui` includes:
  - unit tests for the view-model/reducer (state transitions) using a fake `QueryClient`
  - snapshot tests for non-ANSI text render helpers (headers/rows formatting)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa tui` starts and shows a placeholder screen with nav + help.
- The app uses a shared query client abstraction (no direct DB calls from views).
- Basic error states render cleanly (e.g., “watcher not running”).
